beats.text: You just wrote the same fact two ways. Now I'm going to show you a different rectangle and a different fact. Show me you can do the same thing, two ways around, with a fact you haven't seen yet today. Three quick questions. Take your time.

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected one correct orientation but not both, no incorrect options

params.description: Rectangle pulses to draw attention to the fact.

steps.text: You found one way to write this fact. Look at your answer again. Can you write the same fact with the product on the other side of the equal sign?

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected only incorrect options, no correct orientations

params.description: Rectangle highlights to emphasize the multiplication fact.

steps.text: Think about what the rectangle shows. It is 5 rows with 8 in each row. That means 5 times 8. What does that equal?

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected at least one correct orientation and at least one incorrect option

steps.text: You found at least one correct equation. But check the others carefully. Does the rectangle show addition? Does 8 times 40 equal 5?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Options 5 × 8 = 40 and 40 = 5 × 8 highlight as correct answers.

steps.text: The rectangle shows 5 times 8 equals 40. That is one equation. The equal sign means both sides have the same value, so I can also write 40 equals 5 times 8. Those are the two correct equations.

incorrects.description: Student filled one or more slots incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Check your work. Count the rows and count across one row. What multiplication fact does the rectangle show?

incorrects.description: Student filled one or more slots incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Rows highlight, then one row highlights to show the count across.

steps.text: Count the rows: 7 rows. Count across one row: 6 squares. So the fact is 7 times 6. What does that equal? Then write the same fact with the product first.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Rectangle highlights rows, then columns, showing 7 × 6 = 42 and 42 = 7 × 6.

steps.text: I count 7 rows. I count 6 squares across. That is 7 times 6 equals 42. The equal sign means both sides have the same value, so I can write it the other way: 42 equals 7 times 6. Both equations name the same rectangle.

incorrects.description: Student filled one or more slots incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Look closely. Count the rows and the squares in each row. What multiplication does this rectangle show?

incorrects.description: Student filled one or more slots incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Rows highlight one at a time to help student count, then one row highlights to show the count across.

steps.text: Count the rows: 9 rows. Count the squares across: 4 in each row. So it is 9 times 4. What is the product? Then fill in the same fact starting with the product.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Rectangle highlights to show 9 rows of 4, demonstrating 9 × 4 = 36 and 36 = 9 × 4.

steps.text: I count 9 rows. Each row has 4 squares. That is 9 times 4 equals 36. Because the equal sign means both sides match, I can write the same fact another way: 36 equals 9 times 4. The same rectangle shows both equations.
